Hi, welcome back to Straight Line Persuasion to module three, Tonality. This is where the journey really begins here, because tonality, if anything, is the most important thing that you can learn to master when it comes to influencing and persuading another human being. I look at it as this, the almost the hidden language of influence. You say a few words, but attached to those words based on your tonality is a much more powerful meaning that registers with someone on an emotional, not a logical and emotional basis. So what you're going to learn here, in fact, are 29 different tonal patterns, and you're saying 29, how could I learn that? Don't worry about it. You'll learn to master this very, very easily. It'll become second nature to you. It'll become unconsciously competent. And I've been through this thousands of times, as I said before, and the tonality aspect, while it's the easiest to master, it is the most powerful tool you can ever learn as an influencer. So again, I want you to make sure you take specific actions here as you're watching, right in your workbook. Make those notes and make sure you play full out with this module. And you're going to see here, as I go through it, how in a script, and we always have a script, by the way, even if it's not in front of us, if you're in person, the script is in your head. You're going to learn how in the first few seconds, with a script in your head or on paper, using specific tonalities, you're going to establish those very powerful things that you're an expert, that your shop is a tap, that your enthusiastic is all hell, and most importantly, that you care. See, at the end of the day, people will do business with people they like and they know that care, not just someone with necessarily the best product. Now, of course, you want to have the best product, too, but more importantly is to develop that unconscious connection, what we call rapport. But rapport is not about talking about the price of tea in China, if he mentions China. Repor happens on an unconscious level through things like pacing and leading, which you'll learn about, things called matching and mirroring. These are the unconscious ways of developing rapport that actually set you up to close the sale, to influence somebody down the road. See the tricks here, and almost they are tricks by that, I don't mean in a bad way, what they are is these little sort of unconscious triggers that bypass the logical mind and allow you to talk to someone directly to their heart. At the end of the day, it's really the emotion that you create in somebody that truly closes the deal. The logical part has to be there, too, and you'll see them, a big believer in creating logical cases as well. But at the end of the day, people will buy based on emotion and they'll justify with logic. It begins with tonality, it begins with your ability to create specific emotions in the prospect at specific times, and you'll learn what those times are, and most importantly, to develop that trust, that unconscious rapport, and to use the same tonal patterns, not just for the open, but for the close and every step in between. That's the bottom line. So remember, play full out here. I want you to take the workbook out if you haven't taken it out already, and go through this one with an eagle eye here, and also with your ears wide open, and you're going to practice your tonal patterns, and you will see this instantly, in fact, tomorrow, as soon as you go out there and use this, you will be absolutely shocked at the impact this has on your ability to persuade and influence. So go to it, and I'll see you soon. Okay, this script, okay, pretend for a second we're going to play full out that this is the script you're trading for for your business, okay? And it's a very simple script, okay? It says, you know, hi is John there. Hi, John, this is Jordan Belfer calling from Global Capital in Tampa, Florida. How's it going today? Great. Now, if you recall, you attended a seminar last Thursday night over at the Marriott Hotel, we went to our top traders, James Arnell, Zarringabel, blah, blah, blah, right? Now, if you read the words on the script that way, you've destroyed yourself. You have destroyed yourself. I'm going to show you a way to read the words on this script. So literally, by the time that you are done, not only have you established these three things, like to the point where the person is hanging on your every word, but you've also established that you care about them, that you're a reasonable person, that you're trustworthy, that you're empathetic, that you have the best interest, all that in one paragraph. Now, you're saying, well, you can't do old patent one paragraph. Yes, you can. Yes, you can. Now, is it established to the point where they're like, just do whatever you say? No. What you're doing in this paragraph is you're laying the groundwork. You're pacing and leading them. It's called pacing and leading. You're laying the groundwork for these people to follow you throughout a 10, 15, or 30 minute sale. The sale can be stretched out to three days, and it can be for a $2 million product at the highest level of sales. The system doesn't have to be like a one call close. It could be a five call close if that's what is needed for your product. It could be the longest relationship sale at the highest level of business. It could be about closing a $50 billion deal for a takeover. Doesn't matter. It all starts here. The tonal patterns that you're going to use here in this first paragraph are going to translate to the entire sale to everything you do even after the sales close. You're going to train yourself to speak like influencers, not like average people. If you want to speak like an average person, you're going to be average. There's a different level above average. It's extraordinary. And in sales, to be extraordinary, you need to master the art of tonality, how to speak to people, so you capture their imagination in a second. How do you create scarcity? You're out saying, it's scarce, it's scarce. You don't create scarcity like that. Scarcity is with a tone of voice and some appropriate words and a pattern of language. But tonality is what really makes something seem scarce or something that's a secret. Something that's really, really important, like when I lower my voice. Now you all do this right now anyway, but you're not doing it in a very deliberate way. What we're going to do the next hour is we're going to get really, really deliberate with tonality to the point where you're like, oh my, you're going to be, oh my God. I did not know the emotions I can create in people by lowering my voice or raising up at the end of a sentence. You're going to start to see how powerful this stuff is and you're going to realize what you're going to do is you're going to realize that, hey, if I do that, they're not going to close right now. But if I do this through the entire sale and I have the right structure and I can crack through the limiting buying beliefs, make them trust me as they should, make them trust my company as they should. And by the way, if you're working for a crappy company with a crappy product, my advice, get out now. Get out now because you're going to sell it because you'll take the system, you'll make a fortune, you'll hurt people. Okay? You've got to be selling a great product and working for a great company. I think you all are. I want to show you how literally every word in one paragraph and every sentence into itself, how the words are spaced out, your pacing, the pauses, every little thing in this one paragraph matters immensely. It's hugely important. And you're going to see how you're going to capture their imagination and develop these three things literally in four seconds flat. And then in the first paragraph, by the time you're done, they'll be reinforced that much more. So what I want to show you right now is how you're going to capture somebody's attention in literally four seconds flat. And we're going to play full out here. I want two volunteers from the audience. What we're going to do now is this. Now, if you notice, I wrote this script, wrote it about eight months ago. I know it by heart, but you're going to watch that I'm going to hold the script and I'm actually going to read from the script whenever I do this. Because I'm telling you, the key to success in sales is you take out as many variables as you can from the equation. You control what you can control because there's a lot of stuff you can't control. Okay, so if I have a script that's perfect, and I know the script is perfect because it's been tested and it works, this script will convert everybody who is convertible as long as you say the words the right way. Now I want you to be very conscious. I was going to run an exercise and I was going to start off with an imperfect script and show you how hard it is to use a script that's imperfect. I wrote the first generation was imperfect. In other words, when you're writing scripts, you're writing scripts for your company, a lot of you write scripts. Remember that a script is a work in progress until it gets to the point when it's perfect. When you know it's perfect and it's refined, it's been tested, then you lock it in. When you're going about writing a script, remember that when you write words down, it's slightly different than the spoken word. Certain words are contracted, certain words are dropped completely in conversational English. Most importantly, you sound like a novice by not contracting words and you sound like a stranger. The idea is that when you speak to somebody on the phone, even though you don't know them and they don't know you, you want to capture it in the familiar. That you're a friend to friend. It's not like, hello, Mr. Jones, this is Jordan Belfort. How are you today? He's like, what the hell? Who's this salesman calling me? That's the first thing they think. When you pick up the phone to call somebody, now pretend right now we're going to run this exercise right here as if we're going to try to be selling seminars. Someone actually in this room is selling seminars. It's actually you. Perfect. Perfect. Now, here's what we're going to do. I want you to listen to how I read this script. I'm going to read this script with a specific tonality, which is the right tonality, which is going to capture every single person in the first couple of seconds. You understand that when you say that, they realize you have something. If you say it right, they know you have something important because you're enthusiastic. Don't kid yourself. The picture is forming. Okay? Hi, is John there? I'm excited. I'm upbeat. It's critical, mission critical for any person on the phone. Okay? In fact, I'll tell you a story because it goes to sales in person too. And ultimately, we do meet most of our customers at one time or another. Okay? Or we should, okay, if it's possible. I was in the mortgage business. I made a fortune in the mortgage business. After I got in trouble with the stocks, as you know, I was going to have to go to jail. I spent 22 months in jail. And the fact that I have the life I have now where I go around the world and speak to massive crowds is a miracle into itself. And it really, it was a long way back to this point right now. And a lot of struggle. And I sincerely hope that most of you will never have to know the crap that I went through. Being separated from my kids is awful. It really, really, I hope you cannot empathize with me because it was awful. Okay? But there was a window after I got in trouble and before I went to jail where I actually started a mortgage company and I built into a huge company like in a year, which was not that difficult in 2001, depending on where you know we're in the mortgage business. It was very easy to do. I built it very quickly and I came up with this idea to go door to door for mortgages because I used to be, I start off in door to door sales, my first thing. I said, I could walk down the street when rates had just dropped. I could walk down the street to any store and the business owner must own a house. I would say, hey, what rate are you paying? And the guy would say, I'm paying eight and a half. Really? I could save you a ton of money. How hard is that to do, right? So I came up with that idea and I wrote this little script and I said to my partner, his wife said, come on, you come with me, okay? And we're going to go up to some place. Now, understand this. I want you to learn a lesson here. I had already was making $50 million a year for many years. I was in newspapers all the time. I was well known as the Wolf on Wall Street, all this crap, right? And you would think that I was too proud maybe at the age of 40 to go knocking on doors. I was banging on doors till my knuckles fled. Never ask a salesperson to do something that you wouldn't do yourself. They won't respect you. They won't listen to you. So you can't possibly perform as a manager, as a trainer at the highest level unless you have done it yourself. I think one reason you listen to me, you guys know I'm the real deal. This is not theory. I use this to make hundreds of millions of dollars and turn morons into closers. It works. It works. So I took my partner's wife, said listen, I gave her a quick train. I said, I'm going to go down the right side of the street and you go down the left side of the street. She's like, okay, great. She's a really nice girl. So she went to New Rochelle. I think hopefully no one will recognize me when I walk in the door. So I go like 30 miles away. I have a little bit of pride left. So I go down. The first person I walk in, it's like a diner. If you are a salesperson and you're hammering away, you got people on the phone, a big crew of people, just that alone will increase you up this much. You're not going to close sales, but no. But this is called the art of ready for this. This is a great lesson. This is called, okay, this is called the art, I can't write, the author too, the art of not blowing it. That's what this is. Here's the mistake so many people make. Okay? If this is your straight line, okay, and you're qualifying over here and then you're presenting here and you're closing here, right? Okay? The mistake people make, they think the sales starts here. When they start, the sales starts the moment you pick up your phone, you're closing with your tone. You're closing by establishing the three magic bullets that I am. What? That's number one. What's the attack? Two, what? Enthusiastic as hell. I can't write, you see this? Enthusiastic as hell. B, I am a force, a force to be reckoned with, an authority figure, an expert in my field. That's how I got upstairs to Jacob's office. That's how I made $3 million a year from a dead stop after the government had taken all my money and deservedly so because I had a company, let me pay you something. I invented the straight line at a frustration of trying to take 19-year-old kids with little education and make them into killers who could talk to the wealthiest men and women in America and extract money from them. Not $5,000 millions. We would call people all across the country and they would send us, one guy would send us $4 million, $4 million for a stock he never heard of, to a broker he never heard of, to a salesman who usually still had pimples on his face from acne. It's in millions and millions. I raised, I think, like $3 billion in that heyday all because of the straight line. All because of the straight line and it started off with the first word. Now the mistake, here's the thing, 95% of what I did in business was strictly legitimate. Legit, all legit. I was raising money for companies, Steve Madden's shoes. You know that company, right? It's my company. Not only did I take it public, I owned it. 85% of Steve Madden's shoes was mine. Steve was a minority partner. He owned 15%. He came to me looking for money for venture capital. You do venture capital over here, right? Looking for venture capital. I gave him a million dollars. I said, give me, he had one ugly shoe. It was the ugly shoe I'd ever seen. It was called the Mariland, a big chunky Western clog, right? He was like, I got the shoe. I got it. I don't care about the goddamn shoe. How much you need? Million dollars, great. Give me 85% of your company. Here's a million. He's great. Steve was fresh out of rehab. Okay, sold for 22 years now, Steve. Next day I turned around, I sold off 30% of my stake for a million dollars. Get the picture? I was in for free. I owned 55% of Steve Madden's shoes. It was a start-up. The company now is worth over one billion, not million, one billion dollars from that little tiny investment. That was the business that I was in. It was in venture capital. I raised money using the straight line. Well, legal, perfectly legal. Nothing wrong with it. Sales tactics, a lot of pressure. More than there should have been. Not legal, high pressure, not ethical, not illegal. The mistake that I made is that the straight line was so powerful and raised so much money that I could not keep up with the onslaught of buying coming in from around the world. People from not just the U.S. all over the world. But kept coming in and coming in if I was smart, I would have slowed down my business and not hired as many salespeople and I would have made less money up front and be worth five billion dollars right now. But instead I took as much as I could get the buying and just putting more product out there. You know what? I started putting crap out there. I couldn't, the manufacturing couldn't keep up with sales. Stock started to go up artificially. That's how I got in trouble. And I was manipulating stocks just like that. I did not start off wanting to do that. What I'm saying is, reason I'm telling you this story. A lot of you, especially your own companies here, you're going to notice that you're going to put this stuff in place in your business and we're going to sort. They're going to sort quickly. Like you'll see, boom. Just don't let it go out of control. You need to have the other side of the equation. You know what? If it's loan mods or credit repair, okay, you've got to be delivering the service that you promise with the straight line. You can't deliver them crap. The real estate's going to reverse on you and implode and you'll end up like I did, having to start over again. I come back up with the plumber's license. That's what this does and it'll do it for you too. But learn from my mistakes. Okay? Okay? That's the declarative. Now watch this. Okay? Now I'm going to do what's called infer a micro agreement, okay? By raising up my voice at the end. Watch this. Hi, John. This is Jordan Belville calling from Global Capital in Tampa, Florida. Right? Tampa, Florida? Tampa, Florida? When you raise your voice up like that, what you're doing is saying, right? You recall, don't you? This works every time. Okay? Global Capital, upbeat. When you raise your voice at the end of a word, his unconscious dialogue searches for, should I know this? Right? Right? My daughter was the greatest salesman on earth at the age of five. He told me, Dad, you were taking me to the store, right? Right? I'm like, oh, I'm like, yeah, yeah, yeah. She short-circles my brain. Now I know I didn't tell her that she's going for ice cream or a toy, but she goes, you know, shoot, save me. Dad, you're going to the toy, right? Now you can't say to the guy, I'm from Global Capital, right? Right? You seem like a fool. But by raising your voice up at the end of the words, watch, you're inferring two micro agreements in one sentence. He's saying, Global Capital in Tampa, Florida? How's it going today? He's like, okay, you short-circuit, he's like, oh, yeah, I guess I heard of Global Capital in Tampa, Florida. That's what his unconscious dialogue is doing now. It switches. Okay? Now, again, is he going to close because you did this? No. But that same tonality is going to be used about 300 times in the overall script to generate the precise emotion, the precise agreement that you need for him to break his limiting buying beliefs, to know that he can trust you, to know that you care about him. The tonality switches up something in his brain and he tries to recall because he does not want to be thought full. What you're saying is now if you recall, he's like, do I? That split second that you stop him from saying what an asshole allows you to move forward down the straight line. If you miss the opportunity, he's off the line. You get it? He's off the line. Okay? But he maunders his mind going off in some place else. He just wants the end the call. You've blown it, and now you got to play serious catch up ball. Okay? Why is it that you have some people that are top producers? What is it? Is it an accident? It's never an accident. Some people are born closers. What it means is a born closer is they unconsciously master the art of tonality, controlling the sale. So the bottom line is this. You say something my way, let's say we speak to 100 people, and I do it this way. What percentage of the people you think that are closeable will come forward with me? What percentage? Close to 100. If I read it my way, if he reads it his way, he might get 15 or 20 percent of the people to move forward, because he hasn't captured their imagination, and he hasn't established these three things. And if they do move forward, he has a significant uphill battle to fight, because they know he's really not these three things. And now he's got to make such a case, such an airtight logical case, such an airtight emotional case. It's an uphill battle. You're a little bit higher up in terms of your tonality, it's a bit more advanced, but still there's a lot of things that you're not capturing, and it's so easy to do. This is the point I'm making. This is like a learned skill. I don't have some magic bolt that I was born with. Yeah, I got it. But the point is, is it transfers to anybody. I can sit with any one of you for two or three hours, and as long as you will, you're at least in the beginning, you want to have a burning desire, take opportunity, follow the improvement system. Actually, if you have those, anyone, anyone can learn it. The only people that can't are those that don't really care to, they don't care about money, and by the way, just let's just clear this up right now, money matters. And anyone who tells you a dozen is full of crap. They never had any. Money is not evil. Money doesn't corrupt people. Money's a beautiful, wonderful thing. Rich is good. Your middle name should be, I'm rich, Mr. Rich, right? When you're rich, you can do things to help people. You can be the guy that donates the X-ray machine to the hospital, or who builds the wing of the hospital. You can take care of your family, retire the people that you love, have an impact on the world. The most disempowering state is poverty. What can you do when you're in poverty? Look at it this way. There's no amount of poverty you can have in your life to make one other person wealthy. There's no amount of sadness you can have in your life to make one person happy. There's no amount of sickness you can have to make one person healthy. If you want to make the world a happier, healthier, wealthier place, what you do is simple. You get as much of those gifts as you can for yourself and you spread them around. That's called a reframe. You have any limiting beliefs about money, squash them. They're crap. Whatever fed you with this big, someone's fed limiting beliefs that money might corrupt in some way or it's evil. Money's a beautiful, wonderful thing. Now does that mean you're not going to have problems? No, you have more problems when you have rich because you've got more stuff going on. But you get to show up to your problems in style. In a limousine, a nice watch, and a beautiful suit, right? That's what money does. You show up to your problems in style. So wipe that out of your head. Money is beautiful. It's wonderful. Get money and then give it away. Take as much as you need for yourself and then you spread the wealth around. And then more money comes through. That's a state of abundance versus scarcity. I got to grip every penny. How you perceive money in this world and your own goals for money will dictate where you're going with money. Just imagine this way. If you notice money doesn't really matter. Oh, really? Just imagine if you're in a marriage and you say, well, my wife doesn't really matter. How long is she going to stick around? Well, how long is money going to stick around if you don't think it matters? It's going to go like your wife or your husband. Money matters. Closing matters. Because closing, remember the funnel? OK, or the only problem you're going to have is I didn't call you six months ago and do this for you then. Sound fair enough? You said there's so much. I care about you. I care about your family. I'm going to help you put control on your life. And I am an expert. They all want to deal with the expert. This is what tonality, the straight line is tonality and structure. Tonality and structure to move somebody's emotions down the line with the appropriate tonality, to have them right where you want them, not in an evil, manipulative way, in a way that empowers them to make a good decision. You will never use this to manipulate somebody because now I think you can start seeing you could use it to manipulate someone to make a bad decision, especially when you learn the art of looping, which we'll go through later, which is crazy, OK, where you break their buying beliefs down, OK? So you do this only to empower people. That's the message here, OK? Let's move forward here and say, great. Now, if you're real, let's do it again, from the top. Hi, it's John there. Hi, John there. Let's give these to a big round of applause. Thanks very much. Excellent. Thank you very much. So what do you think? Tonality, right? It's the key to everything. When you lower your voice, people will listen. If you raise your voice, it has some power, but only for a short while. When you really want to influence somebody, you whisper. You have scarcity, secrecy, and people will really respond to that. Now take this to the streets, as I said, and I want you to go out there and act on this one. This is something that you don't have to really think that much about. Just in your mind, just be conscious that there are certain key moments where you pause. There are certain times when you group your words together and become a person that speaks like an influencer. So I tell a story about how a guy at the checkout line is working the cash register. Well, he's a salesperson, too. He's selling people cans of Campbell soup and hairbrushes and toothpaste, right? He gets paid what's been in wages, $6, $7 an hour. That's what you get paid for transactional sales. As an influencer, using your tonality and the structure of the straight line, the amount of money you make could be staggering. So I just want to make sure that's what you took out of that session. What we're talking about here is becoming an extraordinary influencer. And at the heart of that is your tonality. And if you're in person, also remember your body language. Remember the eye contact, how important that is. Shaking hands in an authoritative way, but not shaking someone like a Randall, because people don't respond to that sort of stuff. But again, the key here is to take specific actions in the real world to go out there. Not just conceptually, but when you speak to people, speak to them like an influencer. And remember that kill zone, that sort of 1% inflection that makes the real difference is that you're going to raise your voice up in a certain time to inspire someone to start searching their memory for something so they don't start working against you with their unconscious dialogue. All those little tools and techniques that you just heard about, they will be remarkably effective, not just in your business life, but in your entire life. Whether it's with your spouse, and I urge you by the way with a spouse or a loved one, only use it to empower them, to make them feel more confident about themselves. Don't work against people like that. And at the heart of this whole thing, remember it's ethics and integrity to influence people who should be influenced, because when they do make the decision finally, it was in their best interest and it empowers them and help them get propelled to wealth and success.
